questions.question: According to the lesson, what is the evidence grade for technology's role in legacy preservation?

options.text: STRONG evidence - digital legacy work is well-researched
EMERGING evidence - technology as tool lacks standalone research validation
MODERATE-HIGH evidence - online platforms are proven effective
No evidence - digital legacy work is entirely speculative

questions.explanation: The evidence grade is EMERGING for digital legacy preservation specifically, with MODERATE evidence for digital storytelling in narrative identity contexts and LOW-MODERATE for online wisdom-sharing platforms. The core evidence remains rooted in life review, narrative identity, and generativity research—technology serves as a delivery mechanism that can support these evidence-backed practices, but lacks standalone research validation. Technology should amplify what already works, not replace evidence-based practices.

questions.question: What does the lesson identify as technology's primary role in wisdom-sharing and legacy work?

options.text: Technology replaces in-person connection with more efficient digital methods
Technology amplifies evidence-backed practices like life review and narrative work without replacing human connection
Technology creates wisdom through advanced algorithms and AI
Technology is necessary for all modern legacy work

questions.explanation: The lesson's core principle: 'Technology as Amplifier, Not Substitute.' The most successful digital legacy work uses technology to amplify what already works—structured reflection, meaningful storytelling, genuine contribution, human connection. Technology scales reach, preserves content, increases accessibility, but it doesn't create wisdom, replace presence, or substitute for relational depth. The biggest risk is technology replacing connection instead of enhancing it.

questions.question: For long-term digital preservation (30+ years), what strategy does the lesson recommend?

options.text: Just upload everything to Google Drive—cloud storage lasts forever
Archival services + redundant backups + durable formats (PDF/plain text) + physical backups + designated successor with preservation plan
Social media platforms automatically preserve your content permanently
External hard drives alone are sufficient for generational preservation

questions.explanation: Long-term preservation (30+ years) requires: archival services (Internet Archive, Forever.com, library collections), redundant backups across multiple systems, plain text/PDF formats (most durable over time), physical backups (printed books, microfilm), and a designated successor responsible for ongoing preservation with a written preservation plan. The lesson warns that digital decay is real—file formats become obsolete, cloud services shut down, hard drives fail, accounts get deleted. Without active preservation maintenance every few years, digital content may not outlast physical books.

questions.question: According to the '80/20 Rule for Technology' mentioned in the lesson, what is the recommended approach?

options.text: Use 80% of available technologies to maximize reach
Spend 80% of your time learning new platforms
Use the 20% of technology that provides 80% of the benefit; deep competence in 1-2 simple tools beats shallow dabbling in many
Make 80% of your content digital and 20% analog

questions.explanation: The 80/20 Rule: Use the 20% of technology that provides 80% of the benefit, and stay analog for the rest. Practical application: Choose 1-2 technologies maximum and develop deep competence in simple tools rather than shallow dabbling in many platforms. Technology that's too complex becomes a burden you abandon, so simple and sustainable tech beats ambitious tech that collapses. Quality over quantity. This prevents technology overwhelm and ensures sustainable long-term use.

questions.question: When does the lesson suggest choosing analog approaches over digital for legacy work?

options.text: Never - digital is always superior for preservation and reach
When presence matters most, simplicity serves, tangibility adds meaning, or technology creates distraction/barriers
Only when you lack technical skills
Only for people over age 70

questions.explanation: The lesson explicitly gives 'Permission to Stay Analog': Choose analog when presence matters most (deep 1-on-1 mentoring, trust-building), simplicity serves (not everyone is tech-savvy), tangibility adds meaning (handwritten letters carry different emotional weight), or technology creates distraction (in-person retreats lose power when screen-mediated). If technology feels burdensome, distracting, inauthentic, or excludes your intended audience—stay analog. Handwritten letters, in-person mentoring, printed albums, and face-to-face storytelling are legitimate, powerful legacy paths. Technology is optional, not mandatory.

title: Technology Integration and Digital Legacy Preservation - Quiz
